questions.question: Explain the concept of multiple stacks and mention one scenario where they are useful.
keyPoints: Multiple stacks refer to implementation of two or more stacks in a single array or using linked lists
They are useful in evaluating recursive expressions
Other scenarios: managing multiple function calls, parenthesis matching in compilers

questions.question: What is a circular queue? State two advantages of circular queue over linear queue.
keyPoints: Circular queue is a linear queue where last position connects back to first, forming a circle
Advantage 1: Efficient space utilization - reuses empty spaces
Advantage 2: No wastage of front space after dequeuing
Both enqueue and dequeue operations are O(1)

questions.question: Write the algorithm for enqueue operation in a circular queue.
keyPoints: Check if queue is full: if (rear+1)%MAXSIZE == front, then overflow
Otherwise, if queue is empty, set front = rear = 0
Else, set rear = (rear+1)%MAXSIZE
Insert element at rear position

questions.question: Define DEQUE. Differentiate between input-restricted and output-restricted deque.
keyPoints: DEQUE (Double Ended Queue) allows insertion and deletion at both ends
Input-restricted deque: insertion allowed at only one end, deletion from both ends
Output-restricted deque: deletion allowed at only one end, insertion at both ends

questions.question: Explain overflow and underflow conditions in multiple stacks.
keyPoints: Overflow: Attempting to push into a stack that has reached its limit
Underflow: Attempting to pop from an empty stack
In multiple stacks using array: overflow in one stack may affect others if shared boundaries
In linked representation: overflow only when physical memory is exhausted

questions.question: Write the algorithm for dequeue operation in a circular queue.
keyPoints: Check if queue is empty: if front == -1, then underflow
Otherwise, retrieve element at front position
If front == rear (single element), set front = rear = -1
Else, set front = (front+1)%MAXSIZE

questions.question: Discuss the linked representation approach for multiple stacks.
keyPoints: Each stack is represented as a separate linked list
Nodes are dynamically allocated from heap memory
Each stack has its own top pointer
Advantages: No overflow until physical memory exhausted, stacks grow independently
Disadvantage: Extra memory for storing pointers

questions.question: Explain the representation of K stacks in a single array using the divide-and-conquer method. Write the algorithms for push and pop operations on stack i. Also discuss the overflow condition.
keyPoints: Divide array of size N into K parts for K stacks
Stack i has boundaries: lower = i*(N/K), upper = ((i+1)*N/K)-1
Push: Check if top[i] < upper bound, then increment and push
Pop: Check if top[i] > lower bound, then pop and decrement
Overflow: When top[i] reaches its upper boundary, even if free space exists elsewhere
This method may cause false overflow when one stack is full but others have space

questions.question: Explain the concept of priority queue. Discuss different types of priority queues and their applications.
keyPoints: Priority queue: Elements are dequeued based on priority, not FIFO
Types: Ascending priority queue (min-priority) and descending priority queue (max-priority)
Implementation methods: Array, Linked List, Heap
Applications: CPU scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, Huffman coding, Print queue management
Time complexity: Insertion O(n) or O(log n), Deletion O(1) or O(log n) depending on implementation

questions.question: Write a program to implement a circular queue with operations: enqueue, dequeue, display, isFull, isEmpty. Explain each function.
keyPoints: Define MAXSIZE, front and rear initialized to -1
isFull: returns true if (rear+1)%MAXSIZE == front
isEmpty: returns true if front == -1
Enqueue: Check isFull, increment rear using modulo, insert element
Dequeue: Check isEmpty, retrieve element, increment front using modulo
Display: Traverse from front to rear using modulo

questions.question: Discuss various operations that can be performed on a deque (double-ended queue). Explain input-restricted and output-restricted deques with examples.
keyPoints: Operations: insertFront, insertRear, deleteFront, deleteRear, getFront, getRear
Input-restricted deque: Insertion only at one end (say rear), deletion at both ends. Used in palindrome checking
Output-restricted deque: Deletion only at one end (say front), insertion at both ends. Used in CPU scheduling
Example of input-restricted: Can add only to rear, but can remove from front or rear
Example of output-restricted: Can add at front or rear, but remove only from front
